TextENGNew: ...My head was pounding.
...The sky... was blinding.
changeReason: Japanese 眩しい means 'dazzling/blinding' rather than merely 'bright'; the intensity of the sensation differs.

TextENGNew: ...It was cold.@k...And it hurt.
One sensation after another... each awakening the next in succession.
When I came to... I was lying facedown on a riverbed.
...My entire body screamed with pain.
changeReason: Japanese 全身のあちこちが means 'all over my entire body'; old translation's 'a few body parts' significantly understates the extent of pain.

TextENGNew: The skin over countless joints was torn, bruised purple and red, oozing blood.
...From the searing agony that shot through me with every movement, I could tell I'd injured bones throughout my body.
...I looked up and gauged the height of the bridge I'd fallen from.
...Considering how far I'd dropped... waking up at all was a miracle.
Right beside me... was a broken car seat, ripped from its frame and flipped upside down—probably dragged here by some kids playing around.
...Perhaps it had been conveniently positioned beneath me when I fell, breaking my impact and saving me from instant death.@k...A miracle, if you could call it that.
Of course, whether this miracle was a stroke of fortune or misfortune... I couldn't say.
Having failed to die... I was still trapped in this insane world.
...How long had I been unconscious here?
...The sun was still high—it looked like only an hour or two had passed.
...But the leaden exhaustion in my body made it hard to believe only that much time had elapsed.
...Mentally, it felt as though I'd been asleep here for ten years.
The more feeling returned to my body... the more my pain receptors blazed to life.
...It hurt so much I wished I hadn't woken up at all.
...I needed to see a doctor.@k...Right—Coach's clinic...
The words 'Coach's clinic' triggered a cascade of memories... memories I would have been better off not recalling.
Satoko was no longer on the bridge.
...She'd surely gone back to Hinamizawa by now.
She'd get dressed, go to the clinic, see the doctor... and tell them something was wrong with me.
changeReason: Old renders 俺の様子がおかしい as 'how crazy I had seemed' which adds a judgmental characterization; JPN more neutrally says 'something was off about me.'

TextENGNew: The police were undoubtedly waiting for me.
Would I finally be caught for killing the uncle? Or would they send me to a psychiatric ward this time?
...But... I didn't care which it was.
Just make this pain stop.
...After that, they could do whatever they wanted with me.
Dragging my twisted ankle... I began to hobble forward.
I followed the stream downhill... pushed through forest paths barely wider than animal trails...@kand walked aimlessly in search of a road I recognized.
...Eventually... I stumbled onto a familiar path... and headed toward the clinic.
...Not home... but the clinic—the place that had treated me as a lunatic.
...It was stiflingly hot.
...There was no wind, and the air hung stagnant.
For a while now... a foul odor like scorched eggs had been clinging stubbornly to my nostrils, making me grimace.
...And then, at last, I noticed something.
There were no cicadas.
I'd never known Hinamizawa to be this quiet.
...When I thought about it, Hinamizawa was always alive with sound—birds singing at dawn, cicadas roaring through the day, higurashi cicadas chorusing at dusk.
Not a single insect could be heard.
The only sound was the dry rustle of wind scraping through the treetops.
...I'd never experienced silence like this.
Even the trees—the sole source of sound—seemed listless.
They had turned yellow, shedding an unseasonable carpet of fallen leaves across the road.
...Even the sturdy weeds that should have been thriving along the roadside had yellowed or browned, their vigor drained.
...Only the sunlight was the June Hinamizawa I knew.@k...It was as though an entire season had shifted out of place.
Peering at the fallen leaves and withering weeds, I noticed several small insects lying on their backs, motionless.@k...Dead.
They were corpses.
...On closer inspection, these tiny corpses were scattered everywhere... as if a child had dumped an insect collection across the ground.
...What was this stench...?
A terrible smell... like scorched eggs.
...Paired with all the dead insects and the unseasonable leaves falling from the trees.
...Had someone sprayed herbicide or pesticide?
...Even at school, they fumigate once or twice a year—using chemicals to drive out insects...
Despite how bright it was, I hadn't encountered a single person for a while now.
It was the middle of the day... and yet I felt as though I were wandering through the dead of night.
Well, with this stench filling the air, nobody would want to be outside.
...What on earth... was going on...?
...No sound, no noise, not even the breath of life... a silent Hinamizawa.
Past this bend... the school should come into view.
...And yet I couldn't hear any commotion at all.@k...None of the shrieks or chatter that children always made.
...It was simply... quiet.
Just as I was finding it unbearable to keep pretending the silence didn't bother me... the school came into view.
And finally, I heard something.
...It was the rumble of several trucks.
Several very tall trucks sat idling in the schoolyard.
About ten workers in rain gear were unloading cargo from the truck beds.
...It was sweltering even in my casual clothes... those rain suits must have been unbearable.
...Oh, right. I remembered.
Our school shared its building with the forestry office.@k...It wasn't unusual for forestry trucks to pull into the schoolyard for work.
...Come to think of it, what exactly did forestry service workers do?
...I felt sorry for them, having to work in this heat, surrounded by that awful stench.
They were hauling brightly colored cargo from the trucks and lining it up across the schoolyard.
The loads were fairly large... wrapped in colorful, patchwork-like bags.
They were heavy, too—each one required two people to carry.
They lined them up in neat, orderly rows... like tuna at a fish market.
These 'tuna'—dozens, hundreds of them—were packed tightly across the entire breadth of the spacious schoolyard.
...Momentarily forgetting my pain, I stood transfixed by this massive forestry operation I'd never seen before...
As I stood there in a daze at the school gate... the rain-suited workers far across the schoolyard noticed me.
...One of them pointed at me, and they began calling out to each other urgently.
...I figured they'd yell at me for getting in the way of their work, and I turned to leave quickly.
Just then, two trucks pulled up from behind me.
I stepped aside, and they drove into the schoolyard.
...Tarps covered their cargo beds, but I could tell they were fully loaded.
...As they passed, the stench that rolled off them nearly bent my nose out of shape.
...It was different from the scorched-egg smell I'd been noticing—this was something far worse. Like rotten crab innards.
What on earth was that...?
...Why was today nothing but one foul odor after another?
Then, certain white letters printed on the side of the passing truck caught my eye.
'Ground Self-Defense Forces'
...What?
Self-Defense Forces... like the military?@kWhat were they doing in the schoolyard...?
"Hey, you!@k@vS03/00/taiA04002. Where did you come from?!"
Someone suddenly slapped me hard on the shoulder from behind.
I turned to find... an SDF jeep with a canvas top, parked right behind me.
...The person wore a green rain suit of some kind, a gas mask like something from a movie, and had a canister strapped to their back.
...Not an inch of skin was exposed. The getup was deeply unsettling.
"...Where did I come... ow, ow ow..."
When I tried to speak, a wound on my head stretched and stung.
...Seeing me wince, the military personnel exchanged glances.
...I couldn't read their expressions through the masks... but they seemed stunned.
"Are you a resident of Hinamizawa?@k@vS03/00/taiA04004. Can you tell us your address and name?"
"...Well, yeah, I sort of live here.@k@vS03/01/140101097. ...My name is... Maebara, Keiichi."
When I gave my name and address without hesitation, someone watching from the jeep grabbed a radio and started talking into it.

``...Base, do you read?@k@vS03/00/taiA04006. Base, do you read?@k@vS03/00/taiA04007. 402, survivor found.@k@vS03/00/taiA04008. I repeat, 402, survivor found.@k@vS03/00/taiA04009. Location is in front of the forestry office entrance.``

``Survivor is in good health.@k@vS03/00/taiA04011. External injuries observed across the body, but no threat to life.@k@vS03/00/taiA04012. Able to walk on his own.@k@vS03/00/taiA04013. Taking him into protective custody and transporting to headquarters.``
...I'd been prepared to deal with the police... but the Self-Defense Forces...?
...That seemed a bit excessive.
At their urging, I climbed into the back of the jeep.
Then... they told me to put on one of the huge gas masks everyone was wearing.
...I put it on obediently, and a soldier adjusted the straps and bands, cinching it tight.
...It was heavy, restricted my vision, and made it hot and hard to breathe.
...The world seen through the lenses... already felt stripped of all reality.
My own breathing resonated through the mask—haaah, haaah—like the exhalation of some monster.
...I had absolutely no idea what was happening anymore.
Trembling, I asked the soldier adjusting my mask...
"...Um... excuse me.@k@vS03/01/140101100. ...Did something happen...?@k@vS03/01/140101101. ...What day is today...?"
"Where were you?@k@vS03/00/taiB04002. What were you doing?"
...Great, answering my question with more questions...@kI was the one who wanted answers...
"...There's a suspension bridge way back in the mountains... I fell off it.@k@vS03/01/140101104. ...I think I was knocked out."
"Today is June 22nd, 1983.@k@vS03/00/taiB04004. Wednesday."
...I was startled.@k...Satoko had pushed me off on Tuesday, the 21st.
...That meant... I'd been unconscious on that riverbed for a full day.
"...I'm sorry to ask again, but... what happened?@k@vS03/01/140101105. ...Why is the SDF here?@k@vS03/01/140101106. ...What happened in Hinamizawa?"
...Had my question really been that out of touch?
...None of the soldiers would answer me.
The soldier in the driver's seat, the one who'd been on the radio, turned on the car's radio.
...He adjusted the dial and tuned in to a broadcast station.
...The familiar voice of a radio announcer filled the jeep...

``...was conducted at the Prime Minister's residence. In response to questions from the press corps as to whether the failure to swiftly dispatch the SDF for disaster relief directly led to this unprecedented catastrophe, Chief Cabinet Secretary Okuno commented as follows.``
'We believe that following the prefectural governor's official request for deployment, the response was carried out promptly.'
'He stated thus, maintaining that the SDF's response following the request was swift. It was the prefecture's assessment of the situation that was delayed, according to him.'

'When exactly did the governor, who made the request for SDF deployment, recognize this as an unprecedented catastrophe? That remains the central point of contention, and the debate is far from settled.'
'That was Ogata from our news bureau, reporting from the Prime Minister's residence. Now, let us once again review the timeline of the government's response from the moment the disaster struck.'
changeReason: Japanese names the reporter as Ogata (緒方記者); old translation incorrectly renders the name as 'Mr. Okada'.

TextENGNew: In the dead of night, between June 21st and 22nd.
A large-scale disaster struck Hinamizawa Village, Shishibone City.
The details have yet to be fully investigated, but lethal volcanic gas erupted from somewhere within the Hinamizawa Village district.
The gas, being heavier than air, spilled forth and formed a flowing current.
The gas flow traveled downhill along the stream and struck Hinamizawa Village directly, blanketing the entire district over the course of several hours.
It happened in the dead of night.@kBetween 2:00 and 4:00 a.m.
Every home, every household in Hinamizawa Village was affected.
Most residents were asleep, and it is estimated they died without ever becoming aware of the disaster.
The fact that the catastrophe struck while everyone was in bed delayed its discovery.
...And yet, there had been one small chance to detect it.
A newspaper distribution center in Okinomiya had dispatched a vehicle carrying morning editions to the Hinamizawa branch office.
The customary call confirming arrival—a routine part of their operations—never came that morning.
The shop owner called repeatedly, but received no answer.
He sent his eldest son to check on the situation—and lost contact with him as well...
'...It is deeply regrettable. Had the police or fire department been contacted at this point, the outcome could have been significantly better.'
Residents in areas near the Hinamizawa district began flooding 119 with calls, complaining of a rotten-egg stench, dizziness, nausea, and headaches.
The fire department, noting that identical reports were pouring in simultaneously from a confined area, suspected a chemical spill from a truck carrying hazardous materials.
Contact was lost with a patrol car dispatched to the Hinamizawa district. Its last transmission reported a rotten-egg odor.
The local police, suspecting a wide-area disaster caused by lethal gas, notified the prefecture and prefectural police headquarters at 7:15 a.m.

As the governor was on an inspection trip to a neighboring prefecture, the head of the Environmental Disaster Prevention Division assumed temporary command per protocol and established a disaster response headquarters.
However, the governor could not be reached, and the response was delayed again and again.
'As citizens' groups have recently pointed out, these so-called inspection trips with senior officials are nothing more than social outings—drinking parties for all intents and purposes.'

'Sources close to the governor say he'd been drinking all night and was in a dead-drunk stupor when the call from the environmental director came. It took a long while just to sober him up enough to take the phone.'
And so the authorities spent roughly an hour—the time it took for the governor to grasp the situation and sober up—doing nothing but gathering information.
'While the governor bears the primary responsibility, the environmental director, who was to serve as acting commander in the governor's absence, also faces scrutiny for negligence.'
On the advice of the prefectural police, the governor requested a disaster deployment from the Self-Defense Forces.
By the time the Chemical Defense Unit, equipped with gas protection gear, arrived... more than eight hours had elapsed since the disaster began...

'In any case, it was terribly unfortunate that it struck in the middle of the night while everyone was asleep. Had the same disaster occurred during the day, the outcome would have been entirely different.'
Every household in the Hinamizawa Village district was annihilated.
The death toll exceeded one thousand.
The SDF is currently assessing the extent of the damage, and the number of victims is expected to rise further...
'Joining us in the studio as our guest today is Dr. Fujiwara, an expert in volcanic disasters. ...Doctor, is it possible for volcanic gas to erupt in a region with no known volcanic activity?'
'If we're speaking specifically about the immediate vicinity of a volcanic vent, it is not at all unusual.'
'Japan is a volcanic nation with many active volcanoes, and all their vents carry the risk of hazardous gas emissions.'
'So the rotten-egg-smelling gas that struck Hinamizawa is a commonly encountered volcanic gas?'

'Based on the rotten-egg odor, we believe it was likely hydrogen sulfide, one of the more commonly involved gases in volcanic accidents. It is extremely toxic at high concentrations, capable of killing a person within minutes of inhalation.'
'Hinamizawa Village is far removed from any volcanic vent. Can such gas emissions occur in non-volcanic areas as well?'
'The details are still unclear, so I can't make definitive statements... but there are foreign precedents where hot spring regions experienced similar phenomena.'
'Thank you, Doctor. ...We now have an announcement from the Defense Agency. Mr. Morikawa at the Defense Agency?'
'Yes. I'm at the Defense Agency. The agency has just announced preliminary findings from the Ground SDF's Chemical Defense Unit regarding the volcanic gas responsible for the Hinamizawa disaster.'
'Mr. Morikawa, is the gas still erupting and expanding into the surrounding area?'

'According to reports from the SDF's 9th Division currently conducting rescue operations in Hinamizawa, the gas concentration is gradually decreasing. However, levels are still sufficient to pose a lethal threat, and full protective equipment remains mandatory for all personnel.'

'That said, the Hinamizawa Village district remains in a dangerous state, and continued vigilant monitoring is necessary. Government authorities are maintaining their evacuation advisory for surrounding areas.'
'That was Morikawa, reporting from the Defense Agency. Next, let's go to the Okinomiya district, where evacuated residents are being sheltered...'
"...What... is all of this..."
Inside the jeep, rattling and bouncing over the rough road... no one answered my murmur.
An unprecedented catastrophe... had wiped out Hinamizawa... overnight...?
Hinamizawa Village—where was that, anyway?
...It had to be some remote place deep in the mountains, right...?
Place names on the radio were always like that.
...Unfamiliar names... places I'd never heard of...
Wiped out...?
What did 'wiped out' even mean...?
They usually say 'X number dead, X number injured,' don't they...?
How many people survived?
Where did everyone evacuate to...?
Before long, what came into view ahead was... Coach's clinic.
For the first time ever, I saw the flag actually raised on the clinic's flagpole.
...Japan's Rising Sun flag hung limply, as though exhausted...
The clinic's spacious parking lot was filled with SDF tents and imposing military vehicles.
When they saw the jeep approaching, people in white coats and gas masks came running out of the clinic carrying stretchers.
"You're going to be all right.@k@vS03/00/ishaA04002. How do you feel?@k@vS03/00/ishaA04003. Can you walk?"
Because of my 'ow, oww,' they forced me onto a stretcher.
...Someone who appeared to be a doctor was examining me while relaying my condition over a radio.
"Multiple external wounds.@k@vS03/00/ishaA04005. High probability of fractures and/or internal organ damage.@k@vS03/00/ishaA04006. Prioritizing imaging."
At that moment... from the clinic entrance, people lying on stretchers were being carried out, passing me on their way outside.
...From the look of their clothing, they were Hinamizawa residents... victims.
The stretchers were brought to a stop in front of a truck.
...Two soldiers—one at the shoulders, one at the feet—hoisted each body... and tossed it up onto the truck bed, as casually as handling cargo...
"...Wha-... aaahh...... aaaaahhhhhhh..."
...In the truck bed... human bodies... were stacked in heaps.
...The way they were handled, like mere freight... told me they were already dead.
In that instant... those colorful bags packed onto truck beds... lined up across the schoolyard like fish at a market... I finally understood what they were.
Hundreds... of corpses.
...Bodies...@w800. bodies...@w800. bodies...@w800. bodies...@w800. bodies...@w800. bodies...
"You jackasses!!@k@vS03/00/ishaC04002. Don't handle the victims so roughly!@k@vS03/00/ishaC04003. These are people, damn it! Show them some respect!!"
The soldiers carrying the body bags snapped to attention and saluted...
...This insane version of Hinamizawa could just die.
My...@w800. final...@w800. wish...@w800. had once again...@w800. been granted.
...I... didn't understand anything anymore...
...None of it mattered...@k...If my wishes... my curses... came true the very next day...
...Then please, tomorrow morning, let me die.
...And please... bring an end... to this insane night that has never stopped since that day.
"...?!@k@vS03/00/ishaB04002. Hey—he's lost consciousness!!@k@vS03/00/ishaB04003. He's having a seizure!"
"Maebara-kun!@k@vS03/00/ishaC04005. Can you hear me?!@k@vS03/00/ishaC04006. ...There's no life that deserves to die! Stay with us!!"
Suddenly... the world ceased to matter.@k...I felt no interest in the voices calling out to me.
"He's stopped breathing!@k@vS03/00/ishaB04006. Secure the airway!@k@vS03/00/ishaB04007. Starting resuscitation!"
...I could hear a man screaming and crying right beside my ear.
His words, spoken from the heart—that there was no life that deserved to die.
Such an obvious, commonplace sentiment... and yet it soaked deep into my chest.
...Maybe it had been wrong... to take a life, even to save Satoko.
...If this catastrophe... was the price I had to pay for that...
...Then this was too much, Oyashiro-sama.
...Why... why didn't you just punish me alone...?
...Come to think of it... Satoko had said something once.
Oyashiro-sama's true curse... doesn't strike the person directly... it kills the people they hold dear, one by one.
Oyashiro-sama's... curse.
...It was a convenient excuse I told myself at the very end—that it was Oyashiro-sama's curse, not mine, that had destroyed everything.
...I was lying on a stretcher, so this should have been obvious, but...
...In my last moment of clarity, I noticed one thing.
...Yeah...@w800. ...Today...@w800. ...I hadn't heard that extra set of footsteps...@r@w800. ...not even once.
And now... I was free.
The cries of the higurashi—cries I shouldn't have been able to hear—@rspread and filled my mind.
That tearful chorus... @rwas the only funeral march played for me.
Early morning, June 22nd, 1983.
A wide-area disaster struck Hinamizawa Village, Shishibone City.
Volcanic gas—hydrogen sulfide and carbon dioxide—erupted from the Onigafuchi Swamp, one of the district's water sources, and blanketed the entire village.
Over 1,200 victims.
Over 20 missing.
An unprecedented catastrophe that forced the evacuation of approximately 600,000 people from surrounding municipalities.
Subsequent investigations revealed magma pools and hot springs directly beneath Onigafuchi Swamp.@|@y@kFrom these, lethal concentrations of volcanic gas were confirmed to be seeping upward.
In the immediate aftermath, many people drew parallels to local Hinamizawa legends, calling the disaster a 'curse' and fueling the initial chaos.

Hinamizawa Village reportedly had a tradition warning that when a curse was invoked, miasma would rise and destroy the village.@|@y@kScholars noted this likely stemmed from the villagers' ancestral memory of similar volcanic gas incidents.
Some sensationalist tabloids suggested the disaster was an extension of the serial mysterious deaths that had plagued Hinamizawa for several years.

Furthermore, relatives of Hinamizawa residents who had escaped the disaster began reporting health problems one after another and were hospitalized.@|@y@kThey complained of headaches, hallucinations, paranoia, and other symptoms.

On top of that, when several of these relatives committed suicide by bizarre methods, claiming to be possessed by Oyashiro-sama's curse,@|@ythe media sensationalized the 'curse' to an extraordinary degree.
The terrifying specter of deadly gas creeping silently in the night, killing without warning, spread panic across Japan.@|@y@kFear of being 'cursed' became a nationwide phenomenon.
Some even claimed to be possessed, engaging in bizarre behavior.

Most of these cases were attributable to suggestibility fueled by sensationalist reporting.@|@y@kHowever, these Hinamizawa-related symptoms would later become a subject of significant medical interest.
The Hinamizawa Village district, the target of countless rumors and speculation, is currently sealed off, with flights over its airspace prohibited.
The restrictions were nearly lifted once as gas concentrations declined, but when renewed gas emissions were detected that autumn, the entire surrounding area was sealed off once more.
Word is that the village remains frozen in time—traces of daily life still intact, left to decay in an endless slumber...
The sole confirmed survivor was Keiichi Maebara, a male student residing in Hinamizawa Village.
At the time of rescue, he was suffering from respiratory failure due to gas exposure, with incipient pulmonary edema. Desperate resuscitation efforts saved his life.
He is currently hospitalized at a general hospital within the prefecture.
The press has been pressing for access daily, but all visitation has been refused.
...What did he see in Hinamizawa?
What happened between June 21st and 22nd in Hinamizawa?
To this day, he remains silent, unwilling to speak of it...
Late Summer, 2003
An elderly couple residing in Osaka Prefecture@kdiscovered a cassette tape while sorting through the belongings of their deceased son.
Their son (age 47 at time of death) had gone missing in 1995 when his fishing boat capsized.
Eight years later, in 2003, the couple finally began organizing his possessions as a way of finding emotional closure. It was then that the tape in question was discovered.
From the late 1970s through 1989,@kthe deceased had worked as a reporter for a sensationalist photo tabloid. The tape was believed to be from that period of his career.
The tape's label read: 'November 28, 1983 - Keiichi Maebara.'
It is believed to be a recording from an interview with Keiichi Maebara, the sole survivor of the Great Hinamizawa Disaster of June 1983.

As the only person who had survived the disaster and witnessed the mysterious night of June 21st, he attracted enormous public interest at the time. However, all official interview requests were refused.
...For this reason, the discovery of the tape caused a considerable stir, as it was potentially an invaluable record.
...Click.
"...It's really hard to talk about this stuff when I know it's being recorded."
"Don't worry about it.@k@vS03/00/kisya04002. Look, I'll hide the recorder like this—out of sight, out of mind."
(From this point, the recording quality deteriorates sharply.@kDid something get placed over the recorder?)
"Then let's begin.@k@vS03/00/kisya04004. ...Starting with the first question.@k@vS03/00/kisya04005. ...You were found in a completely different location from the village. Can you explain that?"
"...I went behind the shrine... then followed the forest path for a long time...@k@vS03/01/140101113. ...There was a suspension bridge... and I fell off."
"Was this on the night of the disaster—that is, the night spanning the 21st and 22nd?"
"...No.@k@vS03/01/140101117. It was the morning of Tuesday, the 21st.@k@vS03/01/140101118. ...I fell from the bridge and was knocked unconscious."
"Why on earth were you out there?@k@vS03/00/kisya04008. Everyone's wondering—on a day when you should have been going to school, wasn't it the day of the Watanagashi festival?"
"To put it bluntly, there are people who claim you knew the disaster was coming in advance@kand fled to safety. What do you say to that?"
"...Don't be ridiculous.@k@vS03/01/140101121. Please don't make such irresponsible claims."

"I think it's an awful thing to say, too.@k@vS03/00/kisya04012. Don't take offense—just answer honestly.@k@vS03/00/kisya04013. ...You fell from the suspension bridge and landed on that riverbed, right?@k@vS03/00/kisya04014. ...How far is that from the village?"
"...Probably.@k@vS03/01/140101123. ...I don't go there often, so I'm not exactly sure, but... maybe about two or three kilometers?"
"Hahahaha.@k@vS03/00/kisya04016. If you hardly ever go there, why were you there on a weekday morning?@k@vS03/00/kisya04017. ...Well, I suppose it doesn't matter."
"So... about this riverbed where you claim you were unconscious. There's actually a rumor going around that it's 'impossible.' Have you heard about that?"
"...'Impossible'...@k@vS03/01/140101126. ...That word again... 'impossible'..."
(For a while, Keiichi Maebara mutters the word 'impossible' several times under his breath.)
"...Why... would it be impossible?"
"You know the volcanic gas originated from the swamp called Onigafuchi, right?"
"Well, some scholars built a detailed scale model and ran simulations—and they turned up something very interesting."
"The volcanic gas was apparently heavier than air,@k@vS03/00/kisya04021. and as it spread, it followed the contours of the terrain, flowing like water."
"Starting from Onigafuchi Swamp as the source, they simulated how long it would take the gas to blanket the village—and where it would reach."
"...I'm not really following what you're getting at."

"What I'm saying is: if you were truly unconscious on that riverbed, then you spent the entire night engulfed in lethal volcanic gas.@k@vS03/00/kisya04026. And yet you're alive. That's what the scholars are saying is 'impossible.'"
"Let me be more blunt.@k@vS03/00/kisya04029. I believe... you're lying about where you were."
"...During the disaster, you were hiding somewhere safe... and once the gas had thinned out enough, you appeared in the village and were found by the SDF."
(For a while, the reporter needles Keiichi Maebara with questions, but only deep sighs can be heard in response.)
"...Even if that were true... it wouldn't surprise me at this point."
"What do you mean... it wouldn't surprise you?"
"...You said it yourself just now, didn't you?@k@vS03/01/140101135. 'Impossible.'@k@vS03/01/140101136. ...In my Hinamizawa... 'impossible' things were commonplace."
"People who should have been dead were alive.@k@vS03/01/140101138. ...Things like that... weren't rare at all."
"Ahahahahahaha... ha-ha-ha-ha-ha-ha!"
"Ah-hahahahahaha! Ha-ha-ha..."
(Both the reporter and Keiichi Maebara laugh together for a while.)
"All right, Keiichi-kun, let me change the subject.@k@vS03/00/kisya04036. Do you think the Great Disaster could have been the fifth year of Oyashiro-sama's curse?"
"No, I don't think so.@k@vS03/01/140101142. The fifth year's curse was me."
"Hm?@k@vS03/00/kisya04038. Ahahahaha!@k@vS03/00/kisya04039. What do you mean?"
"What do I mean? ...I was just answering your question."
"The disaster... well, you probably won't believe me.@k@vS03/01/140101145. ...It was my doing.@k@vS03/01/140101146. ...I cursed Hinamizawa, and it was destroyed."
"Hahahahaha...@k@vS03/00/kisya04041. That's... quite a claim."
"Quite a claim?@k@vS03/01/140101148. ...That's an interesting way to put it..."

"I killed Takano-san. I killed Coach and Ooishi too.@k@vS03/01/140101150. ...Back then, I believed I could curse anyone. That if I willed it, whoever I cursed would die by morning.@k@vS03/01/140101151. ...The power of the extra footsteps."
"Footsteps...?@k@vS03/00/kisya04043. ...Hahaha, what are those?"
"Have you... never heard footsteps?@k@vS03/01/140101153. ...Tap, tap.@k@vS03/01/140101154. That extra footstep that echoes after you've already stopped walking.@k@vS03/01/140101155. ...Never?"
"Try it today—when you're walking, stop suddenly.@k@vS03/01/140101157. ...It's interesting."
"...If you clearly stopped... and yet you hear one extra footstep...@k@vS03/01/140101159. that's when you know you're being followed.@k@vS03/01/140101160. ...By Oyashiro-sama."
"Ah... ahahahaha!@k@vS03/00/kisya04045. Y-Yes, I'll watch out for that, hahaha..."
"...Am I really... saying something that funny?"

"You've... been doing nothing but laugh since we started...@k@vS03/01/140101163. ...You think I'm crazy, don't you?@k@vS03/01/140101164. ...That's fine. I'm used to it.@k@vS03/01/140101165. ...Coach looked at me the same way."
"Ha, haha.@k@vS03/00/kisya04048. ...That's not true at all.@k@vS03/00/kisya04049. I'm taking this very seriously, I promise."
"No... I can tell.@k@vS03/01/140101166. Your eyes are the same as Coach's."

"...Since that day, I haven't heard the footsteps again.@k@vS03/01/140101168. ...So I suppose I no longer have the power to curse anyone.@k@vS03/01/140101169. ...But let me show you, just as a demonstration."

"...All right. This time, let me choose how you die, shall I?@k@vS03/01/140101172. ...I'll curse you so that you die in a fishing boat accident.@k@vS03/01/140101173. How does that sound?@k@vS03/01/140101174. ...Not exactly an immediate death, I know."
"...If I were at my peak, like back then, you'd be dead by tomorrow morning.@k@vS03/01/140101175. ...But now... without the footsteps to lend me their power... it might take a little while."
"...Ha, hahahahahahahahahahaha.@k@vS03/01/140101177. ...You'd better be careful too.@k@vS03/01/140101178. ...Not of my curse, though."

"Being cursed to death by the likes of me? ...Hahahahahahahahaha-ha-ha-ha-ha! ...Of the footsteps. The extra footstep... that echoes... when you stop.@k@vS03/01/140101181. ...If you hear it... run.@k@vS03/01/140101182. ...That's my... only piece of advice."
"...Ha... hahahahaha...haha... (the voice on the tape trembles, mixed with something that could be laughter or sobbing, and gradually fades away...)"
